mappings.rationaleAI: Drugs as chemicals administered to change physiological state (C), covering sources and development (C.1), general drug action (C.2), absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion (C.3), and classification by organ system of effect (C.4).
Characterizes the four classes of biological macromolecules and small molecules (B), including their roles in the inorganic milieu of living systems (A) and metabolic processes.
Carbohydrates are one of four major biomolecule families with energy storage and structural roles (B.1), including monosaccharides, disaccharides, and polysaccharides.
Nutrition supplies essential macro- and micronutrients, connecting to vitamins as compounds that cannot be synthesized and must be supplied in food (B.8).

Hormones as signaling molecules (B.9), covering general features and the relationship between endocrine and neural regulation (B.9.a), vertebrate hormones (B.9.b), invertebrate neurohormones and pheromones (B.9.c), and plant hormones (B.9.d).

Metabolism as enzyme-catalyzed reactions connects to ATP as the major carrier of chemical energy (B.4), with metabolic pathways including glycolysis, oxidative phosphorylation, and the citric acid cycle.
Lipids are hydrophobic organic compounds functioning in energy storage, membrane structure, and signaling (B.2), including fatty acids, phospholipids, and steroid hormones.
Pharmaceutical drugs used to diagnose, cure, or prevent disease (C), covering drug discovery and development (C.1) and classification by function and biological system (C.4).
Molecular biology centered on nucleic acids and proteins connects to DNA and RNA as nucleic acids participating in vital processes (B.5).
Proteins as amino acid polymers (B.3), spanning enzymes, structural scaffolds, signaling ligands, and transport carriers in vital processes.
Antibiotics are drugs derived from living microorganisms directed against disease organisms (C.5.a), including beta-lactams, tetracyclines, and macrolides.
Nitrogen features in amino acids, nucleic acids, and ATP (A, B.3), connecting the inorganic milieu to organic vital processes, and appears in nearly all drug classes.
Allergy as IgE-mediated hypersensitivity managed through antihistamines and immunotherapy, relevant to drugs affecting the histamine response system (C.4.h.i).
The liver as the primary metabolic hub performs detoxification and drug metabolism (C.3), directly relevant to absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion of drugs.
Digestion involves the gastrointestinal system relevant to drugs affecting the digestive system (C.4.f), including gastric acid regulation and enzymatic processes.
CO2 participates in the inorganic milieu of living systems (A), connecting photosynthesis, respiration, and the carbon cycle in biological contexts.
Biological and chemical warfare agents (E.4), including weaponization of viruses and chemical compounds for combat use.
Ethyl alcohol as a CNS depressant directly relevant to alcohol consumption (D), including pharmacological effects, chronic health risks, and addiction.
Smoking delivers psychoactive nicotine, relevant to drug use and abuse including the nature of addiction and dependence (C.7).
Skeletal muscle tissue relates to drugs affecting smooth and skeletal muscle systems (C.4.b), including neuromuscular junction pharmacology.
RNA as a nucleic acid polymer (B.5.c), with mRNA, rRNA, tRNA, and CRISPR RNA participating in vital processes.
Carbon's bonding versatility underlies all organic chemicals participating in life processes (B), from carbohydrates through nucleic acids.
Oxygen is essential for aerobic respiration in the inorganic milieu of living systems (A), driving cellular metabolism and oxide formation.
Energy as the capacity to do work connects to ATP as the major carrier of chemical energy in vital processes (B.4).
DNA as a nucleic acid encoding genetic information (B.5.b), central to heredity and gene expression in vital processes.
Vaccines confer active acquired immunity, relevant to drugs affecting the immune response system (C.4.h).
Diabetes management involves insulin and other drugs affecting metabolic function, connecting to drugs affecting the cardiovascular system (C.4.a) and endocrine regulation.
